content: Product-market fit (PMF) signals: day-7 retention flattens above 20-30% (consumer) or 80-90% (SaaS), organic word-of-mouth drives >30% of new users, NPS score exceeds 40, users complain when the product is taken away, and weekly usage frequency exceeds 3x. Sean Ellis test: >40% of users say they'd be 'very disappointed' if the product disappeared.

Viral growth loop: user joins -> uses product -> invites others -> new user joins. Viral coefficient K = invites_per_user * conversion_rate. K > 1 means exponential growth (each user brings >1 new user). Dropbox achieved K = 1.2 with its referral program (500MB free storage per referral). Doubling time = ln(2) / ln(K) * cycle_time.

Content growth loop: create SEO content -> rank on Google -> visitors discover product -> some convert -> product usage generates data/content -> more pages to index. HubSpot grew from 0 to 100K+ monthly organic visitors using this loop. Average time to rank a new page on Google page 1 is 6-12 months. Programmatic SEO can scale to 100K+ pages.

Paid acquisition loop: spend $X on ads -> acquire users -> users generate revenue -> reinvest revenue into ads. Sustainable when LTV/CAC > 3 and CAC payback < 12 months. Facebook/Google CPMs range $5-$50 depending on industry. B2B SaaS paid CAC: $200-$500 for SMB, $1,000-$5,000 for mid-market, $10,000-$50,000 for enterprise.

Sales-led growth loop: SDR outreach -> demo -> trial -> close -> expansion. B2B enterprise sales cycle: 3-9 months. Key metrics: meetings booked per SDR per week (10-15), demo-to-trial conversion (30-50%), trial-to-paid conversion (15-30%), average deal size, and sales cycle length. One SDR can support $500K-$1M ARR generation annually.

Unit economics: LTV = ARPU * gross_margin / monthly_churn_rate. For SaaS with $100 ARPU, 80% gross margin, 3% monthly churn: LTV = $100 * 0.80 / 0.03 = $2,667. CAC = total sales & marketing spend / new customers acquired. LTV/CAC ratio >3 indicates healthy unit economics; <1 means you lose money on every customer.

Burn rate = monthly cash outflow - monthly cash inflow. Runway = cash_balance / monthly_burn_rate. A startup with $2M cash and $150K/month burn has 13.3 months runway. Rule of thumb: raise enough to fund 18-24 months of runway. Start fundraising when 6-9 months of runway remain (fundraising takes 3-6 months). Net burn below $50K/month is considered capital-efficient.

Series A benchmark metrics (2024): $1M-$2M ARR minimum, 2.5-3x year-over-year revenue growth, net revenue retention >110%, gross margin >65% (SaaS), monthly revenue churn <2%, LTV/CAC >3, and demonstrable go-to-market repeatability. Top quartile Series A companies show $2M+ ARR with >3x growth and <1% monthly churn.

Pitch deck structure (10-15 slides): 1) Title/one-liner, 2) Problem (pain point with data), 3) Solution (product demo/screenshots), 4) Market size (TAM/SAM/SOM), 5) Business model (pricing, unit economics), 6) Traction (revenue graph, user metrics), 7) Competition (positioning matrix), 8) Team (relevant experience), 9) Financials (projections), 10) Ask (amount, use of funds, milestones).

Growth accounting: MRR_end = MRR_start + new_MRR + expansion_MRR - contraction_MRR - churned_MRR. Net new MRR = new + expansion - contraction - churn. Quick ratio = (new + expansion) / (contraction + churn). A quick ratio >4 indicates excellent growth efficiency; 2-4 is good; <1 means the business is shrinking.

Cohort analysis tracks behavior of user groups acquired in the same period. Month-1 retention of 40% that stabilizes at 15-20% by month-6 indicates a healthy product (consumer). SaaS dollar retention cohorts should show expansion (net retention >100%). If month-12 cohort revenue > month-1 cohort revenue, the product has negative net churn — the holy grail of SaaS.

T2D3 growth framework for SaaS: triple revenue in year 1 and year 2, then double for years 3, 4, and 5. Starting at $1M ARR: $3M -> $9M -> $18M -> $36M -> $72M. Companies achieving T2D3 trajectory typically reach $100M ARR in 7-8 years. Only ~1% of SaaS companies achieve this pace; median SaaS growth is 30-40% annually.

Product-led growth (PLG) metrics: time-to-value <5 minutes (Slack, Notion), free-to-paid conversion rate 2-5% for freemium / 15-25% for free trial, PQL (product-qualified lead) conversion to paid >20%, and expansion revenue >30% of new revenue. PLG companies have 50-70% lower CAC than sales-led peers because the product is the primary acquisition channel.

Magic number measures sales efficiency: (current quarter ARR - previous quarter ARR) * 4 / previous quarter sales & marketing spend. Magic number >1.0 means $1 of S&M generates >$1 of ARR (invest aggressively). Between 0.5-1.0 is moderate efficiency. Below 0.5 signals go-to-market inefficiency requiring optimization before scaling spend.

Net Revenue Retention (NRR) = (beginning_MRR + expansion - contraction - churn) / beginning_MRR * 100. Top SaaS companies achieve NRR >130% (Snowflake 158%, Datadog 130%, Twilio 131%). NRR >100% means the company grows even without acquiring new customers. For every 1% improvement in NRR, company valuation increases ~0.5x ARR multiple.

The Rule of 40 states that revenue growth rate + profit margin should exceed 40%. Example: 60% growth + -10% margin = 50% (passes). 20% growth + 15% margin = 35% (fails). Companies above the Rule of 40 trade at ~12x revenue; those below trade at ~5x revenue. The best SaaS companies achieve Rule of 60+ (Datadog, CrowdStrike).

Activation rate is the percentage of signups who complete the 'aha moment' action. Examples: Slack (sending 2000 team messages), Facebook (adding 7 friends in 10 days), Dropbox (uploading 1 file). Improving activation from 20% to 30% is equivalent to increasing all top-of-funnel acquisition by 50%. Activation is often the highest-leverage growth metric to optimize.

CAC payback period = CAC / (ARPU * gross_margin). For $5,000 CAC, $500/month ARPU, 80% margin: payback = $5,000 / ($500 * 0.80) = 12.5 months. Best-in-class SaaS: <12 months. Acceptable: 12-18 months. Red flag: >24 months. Shorter payback means faster cash recycling into growth. Venture-funded companies can tolerate 18-24 months; bootstrapped need <12.

Pricing strategies: value-based (charge 10-20% of value delivered to customer), competitor-based (match or undercut), cost-plus (cost + margin target). SaaS pricing models: per-seat ($5-$50/user/month for SMB tools), usage-based (Twilio $0.0075/SMS, AWS per-compute-hour), tiered (3-4 tiers, anchoring around mid-tier), and flat-rate (Basecamp $99/month unlimited).

North Star Metric (NSM) is the single metric that best captures the value delivered to customers. Examples: Airbnb (nights booked), Spotify (time spent listening), Slack (daily active users sending messages), Shopify (GMV through platform). A good NSM correlates >0.7 with revenue growth and reflects actual user value, not vanity metrics.

Marketplace metrics: GMV (gross merchandise value), take rate (platform commission 10-30%), liquidity (% of listings that transact within 30 days, target >20%), supply/demand ratio, and time-to-match. Marketplaces face the chicken-and-egg problem: solved by constraining geography (Uber launched city-by-city), subsidizing one side, or single-player mode (OpenTable's reservation tool).

Churn analysis: logo churn (% of customers lost) vs revenue churn (% of MRR lost). Revenue churn is higher when large customers leave; lower when small customers leave. Gross churn rate = churned_MRR / beginning_MRR. SMB SaaS: 3-7% monthly logo churn is typical. Enterprise SaaS: <1% monthly logo churn. Reducing churn from 5% to 3% monthly doubles average customer lifetime from 20 to 33 months.

AARRR pirate metrics funnel: Acquisition (how users find you, measure CAC), Activation (first value experience, measure activation rate), Retention (users come back, measure DAU/MAU or weekly retention), Revenue (users pay, measure ARPU and conversion), Referral (users invite others, measure viral coefficient K). Each stage should be measured independently with cohort analysis.

Burn multiple = net burn / net new ARR. If you burned $5M to add $2.5M net new ARR, burn multiple = 2x. Burn multiple <1x is amazing (efficient growth), 1-2x is good, 2-3x is concerning, >3x is poor capital efficiency. David Sacks coined this metric as a simpler efficiency measure than the magic number. High burn multiples are the #1 predictor of startup death.

TAM (Total Addressable Market) calculation methods: top-down (industry reports, e.g., 'global CRM market is $80B'), bottom-up (number_of_target_customers * annual_contract_value, e.g., 500K SMBs * $2K/year = $1B), and value-theory (total value created * capture rate). VCs prefer bottom-up with credible assumptions. TAM >$1B is typical threshold for VC-backable startups.
